subject: ap-csp
theme: Computing innovations have both beneficial and harmful effects on society — including unintended consequences, bias, privacy risks, and ethical responsibilities — that require thoughtful consideration.
core_concepts: Unintended consequences are effects of computing innovations not anticipated by their creators — social media and mental health is a key example.
Digital divide: unequal access to technology (and the opportunities it enables) across socioeconomic, geographic, or demographic lines.
Computing bias arises when algorithms produce systematically unfair results — often from unrepresentative training data rather than programmer intent.
PII (Personally Identifiable Information) includes any data that can identify a specific individual; combining datasets can create PII from non-PII fields.
Symmetric encryption uses one shared key (key distribution is the vulnerability); public key encryption uses a public/private key pair (public key can be shared openly).
Phishing impersonates trusted entities to steal credentials; malware is malicious software; rogue access points are fraudulent wireless networks.
Creative Commons provides structured permissions between all-rights-reserved copyright and full public domain; open-source software can be reviewed and modified.
exam_tip: Unit 5 is 21–26% of the exam — know the distinction between symmetric and public key encryption, be able to identify phishing vs. malware vs. rogue access points, and recognize unintended consequences vs. computing bias in scenarios.
